statement.markdown: Given a number n, find and print all the prime factors of n. A prime factor is a prime number that can divide n without leaving a remainder.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n.
statement.outputFormat: Print all prime factors of n, one per line.

examples.explanation: The prime factors of 20 are 2, 2, and 5.
The prime factors of 100 are 2, 2, 5, and 5.

hints.text: Start by defining a function to check if a number is prime or not.
Implement a function to check if a number is prime, using a list of primes up to the square root of the number.
Implement a function to find the prime factors of a number by iterating through the primes and checking if they divide the number evenly.
Call the prime factorization function for the input number and print out the prime factors.

editorial.title: Optimizing Prime Factor Printing
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that any factor larger than the square root of n would have a corresponding factor smaller than the square root, allowing us to only check up to the square root.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking every number up to n to see if it's a factor, resulting in a time complexity of O(n), which is highly inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: By iteratively dividing n by the smallest divisor i, we can efficiently find all prime factors.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves a simple loop that checks divisibility up to the square root of n, achieving a time complexity of O(sqrt(n)) and a space complexity of O(1).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness stems from the fact that if n is divisible by i, then n/i is also a factor. By iteratively dividing by the smallest factor, we ensure all prime factors are found. If n > 1 at the end, then n itself is a prime factor.
editorial.dryRun: For example, with n = 12, the algorithm correctly prints 2, 2, 3.
commonMistakes: Not recognizing that checking up to the square root of n is sufficient.
Failing to handle the case where n itself is a prime number.
Incorrectly assuming a more complex algorithm is needed.
Not realizing the space complexity is O(1) due to a constant amount of space used.
Overcomplicating the algorithm with unnecessary data structures.
